diagrams.title: Operator Precedence Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Vertical pyramid showing the hierarchy of regex operators from highest to lowest precedence
elements: Top level: Parentheses () with label 'Highest - Evaluate First'
Second level: Kleene star * with label 'High Precedence'
Third level: Concatenation (implicit, shown as adjacent symbols) with label 'Medium Precedence'
Bottom level: Union | with label 'Lowest - Evaluate Last'
Dotted downward arrows connecting each level
Color gradient from red (highest) to green (lowest)
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand the correct order of evaluation when parsing complex regex expressions like ab*|c = (a(b*))|c

diagrams.title: Thompson's Construction: Basic Building Blocks
diagrams.description: Four separate NFA diagrams showing the base cases for Thompson's construction algorithm
elements: Diagram A: Single character 'a' - state 0 (start) with transition 'a' to state 1 (accept)
Diagram B: Empty string 'ε' - state 0 with ε-transition to state 1 (accept)
Diagram C: Empty set '∅' - single state that is not accepting, no transitions
Diagram D: Union operation (R1|R2) - shows parallel NFAs with new start and accept states connected by ε-transitions
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the foundation for converting any regex to equivalent NFA using recursive construction

diagrams.title: Thompson's Construction: Compound Operations
diagrams.description: Three NFA diagrams showing concatenation and Kleene star constructions
elements: Concatenation (R1R2): Start state of R1 connected to accept state of R1 which has ε-transition to start state of R2, with R2's accept state becoming the final accept
Kleene Star (R*): R's start and accept states connected in a cycle with ε-transitions, new start state with ε to original start and original accept, ε-loop back to start
All diagrams show clear state numbering (0,1,2...) and labeled transitions
diagrams.purpose: Shows students how to build complex NFAs from simpler components using the recursive construction method

diagrams.title: Regex Examples: Pattern Matching Visualization
diagrams.description: Three example regex patterns with visual representation of matched and rejected strings
elements: Example 1: regex 'a*b*' - shows matched: '', 'a', 'aa', 'b', 'ab', 'aab' (green checkmarks), rejected: 'ba', 'aba' (red X marks)
Example 2: regex '(ab)*' - shows matched: '', 'ab', 'abab', rejected: 'a', 'b', 'aba'
Example 3: regex 'a(b|c)*' - shows matched: 'a', 'ab', 'ac', 'abc', 'acb', rejected: 'b', 'c', 'ba'
Each example includes the language set notation in braces
diagrams.purpose: Reinforces understanding by showing concrete examples of what strings each regex pattern matches

diagrams.title: Algebraic Laws of Regular Expressions
diagrams.description: Visual display of the fundamental algebraic identities and laws that regex obey
elements: Commutative: R1|R2 = R2|R1 shown with curved double-headed arrow
Associative: (R1|R2)|R3 = R1|(R2|R3) with grouped parentheses
Distributive: R1(R2|R3) = R1R2|R1R3 and (R1|R2)R3 = R1R3|R2R3
Identities: εR = Rε = R, ∅R = R∅ = ∅
Idempotent: R|R = R
Annihilator: ∅R = R∅ = ∅
Kleene star properties: ε* = ε, ∅* = ε, R** = R*
diagrams.purpose: Provides visual reference for algebraic manipulation of regex expressions used in proofs and simplifications

charts.title: Basic vs Extended Operators Equivalence Table
charts.content: Two-column table mapping each extended operator to its basic operator equivalent: R+ maps to RR*, R? maps to R|ε, [abc] maps to a|b|c, . maps to (a|b|c|...|z), R{n} maps to R repeated n times, R{n,m} maps to union from R^n to R^m, R* maps to R{0,∞}, R{1,∞} maps to R+

charts.title: Regex to NFA Conversion Algorithm
charts.content: Step-by-step flowchart: 1) Parse input regex into parse tree, 2) For each leaf node create basic NFA (a, ε, or ∅), 3) For each internal node apply corresponding construction (union → parallel NFAs with ε-edges, concatenation → connect accept to start, Kleene star → add cycle with ε-edges), 4) Return final NFA with single start and accept state

charts.title: Operator Precedence Examples
charts.content: Three-column table with regex expression, incorrect interpretation, and correct interpretation: 'ab*|c' incorrectly parsed as '(ab)*|c' but correctly as '(a(b*))|c'; 'a|bc' incorrectly as '(a|b)c' but correctly as 'a|(bc)'; '(ab)*c' correctly as '((ab)*)c' due to parentheses forcing evaluation first

charts.title: Regex Operators Quick Reference
charts.content: Five-row table with columns for Symbol, Name, Mathematical Notation, Meaning, and Example: 'a' as Literal matches character a; 'ε' as Empty string matches empty string; '∅' as Empty set matches nothing; 'R1|R2' as Union matches strings from R1 or R2; 'R1R2' as Concatenation matches R1 followed by R2; 'R*' as Kleene Star matches zero or more repetitions of R

branches: Syntax & Operators: literals (a,z,0,9), ε (empty string), ∅ (empty language), union (|), concatenation (implicit), Kleene star (*)
Operator Properties: precedence order (), *, concat, |; algebraic laws (associative, distributive, identities)
Extended Operators: R+ (one or more), R? (zero or one), [abc] (character class), . (any char), {n} (exact count), {n,m} (range)
Conversion to Automata: Thompson's construction algorithm, building blocks, recursive composition, NFA equivalence
Language Representation: describes regular languages, finite vs infinite sets, pattern matching capability
Applications: lexical analysis (compilers), text search (grep), input validation (forms), log parsing, data extraction
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting regex syntax, theoretical properties, conversion methods, and practical applications for complete topic understanding
